rationale: The citing paper uses Berger and Milkman (2012) repeatedly to frame a central prior claim in the literature: emotional content tends to spread and become popular. It then directly positions its own contribution as showing boundary conditions to that pattern in online reviews, including opposite effects for utilitarian products. In Study 8 and the general discussion, the authors explicitly contrast their findings with Berger and Milkman and explain why differences in context (anonymous reviews, trust) can reverse the effect.
relation_description: The citing paper treats Berger and Milkman (2012) as a key prior benchmark on emotion-driven diffusion, then qualifies that generalization by showing that emotional review language can reduce helpfulness and popularity for utilitarian products. This narrows when emotion increases spread by introducing product-type expectations and trust as boundary conditions.
